Renovation · Before & After

The bathroom that taught me patience — an incremental renovation done right

Marcie  ·  April 2026  ·  8 min read

I want to be honest with you about this bathroom. It didn't happen in a weekend. It didn't even happen in a month. It happened in small, quiet moments over a longer stretch of time — a new vanity here, a wallpaper weekend there, a light fixture swap on a Tuesday evening when the kids were finally in bed.

And I think that's actually the most important thing I can share about it.

Because here's the truth about home transformation that nobody's Instagram shows you: most of the best rooms don't happen all at once. They happen incrementally. And incremental is not a compromise — incremental is how you end up with a room that actually reflects you, because you had time to think between each decision.

"Incremental is not a compromise. It's how you end up with a room that actually reflects you — because you had time to think between each decision."

This is the story of my kids' bathroom. Here's where it started.

The before: builder beige and zero personality

Let me paint you a picture. Tan walls — the exact shade of "we gave up." White floor tiles with grout that had seen better decades. A vanity that was technically functional but had the mismatched hardware of a space nobody had looked at intentionally in years. A bare-bulb light bar. A plain mirror. A flamingo shower curtain that I loved but that was doing all the personality work entirely alone.

Before
After

Same room. Same bones. Everything else is different.

The cowboy cat print on the wall — my kids' absolute favorite — was working hard too. But a cowboy cat and a flamingo shower curtain do not a bathroom make. The shell around them needed to catch up.

How it actually happened: the phases

I didn't sit down with a grand plan and execute it in order. This is real life. Here's honestly how it went, phase by phase.

1
The monogram hooks — where it started
The very first thing I did was swap out the towel situation. The original setup was nothing — towels just draped wherever. I found a pair of brass monogram letter hooks — a D and a C, one for each kid — and hung them on the wall near the shower. That was it. That was the whole project that day. Two hooks. But something clicked when I looked at it: the brass was warm, the letters were personal, and suddenly this bathroom felt like it belonged to these specific children and not just any bathroom in any house.
2
The vanity swap
The original vanity had a cultured marble top — that beige-y integrated sink situation that dates a bathroom instantly. I replaced it with a new white vanity with cleaner lines, crystal knobs, and a separate white top. The difference was immediate and significant. White vanities are forgiving, they feel fresh, and they give everything around them room to breathe. I also swapped all the hardware to brushed nickel to match the new faucet.
3
The floor — the biggest visual bang
The old white floor tile had taupe grout that had darkened with age and made the whole floor feel grimy even when it wasn't. I laid peel-and-stick marble-look tile directly over it. This is the project people are most skeptical about until they see it in person. The pattern — large-scale white with soft grey veining — completely modernized the floor without a single tile saw or bag of thinset. It also made the room feel twice as large.
4
Board and batten — the walls get architecture
This was the project that made everything else make sense. I ran board and batten along all four walls at chair-rail height — simple 1×3 MDF verticals with a horizontal cap rail. Painted everything below it in Sherwin-Williams Alabaster. Suddenly the room had structure. The lower half felt intentional and finished. And it created a perfect visual boundary for what was about to happen above it.
5
The navy grasscloth wallpaper — the moment everything clicked
Above the board and batten I hung a deep navy blue grasscloth-look wallpaper. This was the decision I'd been building toward without knowing it. The navy against the white millwork, with the brass hooks and the warm wood tones — it all landed at once. The flamingo shower curtain, which had always felt a little random on the tan walls, suddenly looked completely intentional against the navy. The cowboy cat print, same thing. Everything that had been floating suddenly had a home.
6
The light fixture and mirror — the finishing details
The last two changes were the light fixture (swapped for a two-bulb schoolhouse style in brushed nickel — brighter, cleaner, more charming) and the mirror, which I replaced with a hand-painted blue and white floral frame. That mirror is the piece that makes people stop. It's unexpected. It's a little maximalist. It's exactly right for this room.

The detail that started it all — up close

I keep coming back to those monogram hooks. They were the first thing. They cost almost nothing. And in a strange way they set the direction for everything that came after — because brass, and warmth, and personalization became the throughline of the whole room.

Early on
After

Same brass hooks. Completely different room around them.

Look at those same hooks against the navy grasscloth. The hooks didn't change. The room changed around them. That is what incremental renovation looks like from the inside — you add one right thing, and it starts telling you what the next right thing is.

The lesson

You don't need the whole vision before you start. You need one decision you feel good about. Make that one. Let it show you the next one.

What I'd tell you if you have a bathroom like this

Start with the thing that bugs you most. For me it was the floor — it felt dirty even when it was clean, and that was demoralizing. Fixing it first changed how I felt about the whole room every single day, which made me want to keep going.

Then pick something personal. The monogram hooks cost under $30 combined. But they turned a generic bathroom into their bathroom. That feeling is worth more than any renovation line item.

And if you're doing it in phases like I did — give each phase time to settle before moving to the next. You'll make better decisions, and you'll actually enjoy the process instead of white-knuckling through it.

The finished bathroom — navy grasscloth, board and batten, marble peel-and-stick, brass monogram hooks, schoolhouse light, hand-painted mirror, and one very important cowboy cat.

Everything used in this renovation

  • Peel-and-stick marble-look floor tile (over existing tile)
  • 1×3 MDF boards for board and batten
  • Sherwin-Williams Alabaster (millwork + lower walls)
  • Navy grasscloth peel-and-stick wallpaper (upper walls)
  • New white vanity with crystal knobs
  • Brushed nickel faucet + hardware
  • Two-bulb schoolhouse light fixture, brushed nickel
  • Hand-painted blue and white floral mirror frame
  • Brass monogram letter hooks (D + C) [Anthropology]
  • Woven rattan wastebasket
  • Flamingo shower curtain (the original — kept and earned)
